course_description: Theory and practice of economic policy. Fiscal & monetary policy in open economy. Fixed versus flexible exchange rates. Economic interdependence and international business cycles. Growth and development policy. Money and inflation in Australia. Political business cycles, central bank independence and inflation targeting. Fiscal policy and inflation. Labour markets and unemployment.

requirements.assumed_background: It is assumed that you have previously studied macroeconomics at the intermediate level and that you are therefore familiar with most of these topics: the closed economy IS-LM model, open economy IS-LM-BP macroeconomics, the AD-AS framework, Phillips curve analysis and the neoclassical growth model.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Tutorials for this course begin in Teaching Week 2. Students are required to sign-on for a tutorial via mySI-net. Sign-on will be open from Monday 13th July 10:00am until Thursday 6th August 5:00pm .

Book into your diary NOW the following possible dates for the mid-semester exam: Saturday 5th, 12th and 19th September 2015 . The mid-semester exam is managed centrally by the University. The exact date will be announced by the University in mid-August. Please keep all these dates free of any other commitments for the time being.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The Global Financial Crisis (GFC) challenged some widely accepted macroeconomic policy perspectives that are based upon general equilibrium and efficient market theorising. In this course the development of macroeconomic policy, from its roots in the bitter experience of the Great Depression up to the 'Great Recession' that followed the GFC, is discussed. The GFC made people pay much more attention to longstanding critiques of standard macroeconomic theory and its policy implications. These critiques are examined and assessed. Aside from the implications of different theoretical standpoints, the course also deals in some detail with the actual operation of fiscal and monetary policies and the practical problems and dilemmas that are faced by policymakers.

learning_outcomes.description: Command and use a core, comprehensive and well-founded knowledge of the field of modern macroeconomics.
Better understand and interpret macroeconomic indicators.
Have the ability to define and analyse problems based on models as applied in an institutional context.
Identify key contemporary macroeconomic problems, both domestic and international and discuss possible solutions and potential innovations towards improving current policies and practices.
Collect, analyse, and organise information and ideas independently through wider reading and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently.
Obtain analytical skills and capacity for lateral thinking.
Understand the theory and its assumptions so that better policy recommendations can be made

assessment_details.task_description:
The intention is that the 60 minute exam will cover the first 4 lectures, 4 tutorials and the associated assessable reading materials. Examination will consist of short answer and problem solving questions.

The exam will cover either the first 3 or 4 topics (and associated tutorials), depending on the scheduled date. You will be informed of the coverage once the exam date has been announced.

The mid-semester exam will take place outside the lectures and Tutorial slots, on either of these date: Saturday 5th, 12th or 19th September 2015 (to be advised) , and will be administered by the University. The date is centrally timetabled and outside of the Course Coordinator's control. The exact date will be communicated to the School around mid-August 2015, and students are urged to keep the three dates free of any other commitments for the time being. Students who are working during weekends should already inform their employer about these three dates, as work commitments will not be considered as a valid extenuating circumstance for not attending the exam . The exam will last 60 minutes plus 10 minutes reading time.

Learn.UQ

assessment_details.title: Asset price bubbles
assessment_details.task_description:
Assignment on asset price bubbles

At a speech in Brisbane on 10 June 2015, the Reserve Bank Governor Glenn Stevens referred to Sydney’s “crazy” house prices, suggesting the central bank considers there is a housing price bubble, at least in Sydney.

You are asked to write a short paper on the appropriate policy response to asset price bubbles. The assignment should contain no more than 2,000 words.

The assignment should include:

general overview of asset price bubbles (i.e. what are they and with references to historical examples);

theoretical perspectives on asset price bubbles;

evidence of an asset price bubble in Australian housing—i.e. is there actually a bubble, and what is causing it?; and

if there is a bubble, what if anything should the Reserve Bank (or the Treasury or another agency) do about it?

Imagine you are writing the paper for a feature article in the Australian Financial Review . That is, imagine the reader is a reasonably well-informed business professional, but someone who may need theoretical concepts explained to them and who may struggle with equations and complicated diagrams. In other words, keep it simple, please.

Submission:

Submit via Blackboard/Turnitin.

assessment_details.title: Final Examination
assessment_details.format: Short answer, Short essay, Problem solving
assessment_details.task_description:
The final examination is designed to cover all learning objectives and to test both depth and breadth of students’ knowledge relevant to the course. It will be two hours in duration, plus the usual 10 minutes reading time, and will consist of material dealt with in ALL of the lectures and tutorials.

There will be a set of short-answer and essay questions. Some questions will focus specifically on assigned readings.

learning_resources.required_resources:
At a minimum, you will need to read relevant sections of the textbook:

Dornbusch , R., Bodman , P., Fischer, S. and Startz, R., 2012, Macroeconomics—Third edition , McGraw-Hill.
learning_resources.recommended_resources:
Acemoglu, D. and Robinson, J., 2012, Why Nations Fail: The Origins of Power, Prosperity and Poverty , Profile Books.

Geithner, T., 2014, Stress Test: Reflections on Financial Crises, Random House .

Greenspan, A., 2007, The Age of Turbulence: Adventures in a New World , Penguin.

Krugman, P., 2008, The Return of Depression Economics and the Crisis of 2008, Penguin.

Makin, A., 2010, “Did Australia’s Fiscal Stimulus Counter Recession?: Evidence from the National Accounts”, Agenda, vol. 17, no. 2.

Roubini, N. and Mihm S., 2011, Crisis Economics: A Crash Course in the Future of Finance , Penguin.

Reinhart, C. and Rogoff, K., 2009, This Time is Different: Eight Centuries of Financial Folly , Princeton University Press.

learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
Students are encouraged to read current media articles concerning macroeconomic policy, both in Australia and internationally. Good sources are: The Economist ; The Australian Financial Review; The Financial Times; The Wall Street Journal; Business Spectator (web-based). There are some blogs well worth looking at. For example, those by John Quiggin, Brad De Long, Paul Krugman and MacroBusiness are worth looking at regularly for pieces on macroeconomic policy.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: 1: Overview of macroeconomic policy: (Lecture): What, why, when, how, where and who? Overview of macroeconomic policy targets and instruments, and who manipulates the instruments and under what circumstances. Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 5, 17, 18)

2: Controversies in macroeconomics: (Lecture): Keynesians (old and new), monetarists and new classical economists. Reviews the great debates in macroeconomicsfeaturing Tobin, Friedman, Barro and Lucas, among othersin the post-war period and their implications for macroeconomic policy. Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 1 and 22)

3: The Great Moderation (Lecture): and pre-financial crisis policy perspectives. Considers and critiques the (almost) consensus view on macroeconomic policy that existed during the Great Moderation, prior to the 2008 financial crisis. Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 20); Greenspan (esp. Ch. 19.)

4: The 2008 financial crisis: (Lecture): origins and implications. An overview of the 2008 financial crisis, its causes, costs and implications for macroeconomic theory and policy. Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 20 and 21); Krugman; Roubini

5. Contemporary fiscal policy: (Lecture): theory, evidence and implementation. Reviews theory and evidence on the stance of fiscal policy, the multiplier, and the merits and demerits of activist fiscal policies of various kinds, taking into account the openness of the economy and exchange rate regime. Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 11, 12, 13); Krugman; Makin

6: Contemporary monetary policy: (Lecture): theory, evidence and implementation. Reviews the objectives of central banks such as the Reserve Bank of Australia and US Federal Reserve, how monetary policy is actually implemented, its successes and failures, and including a consideration of non-standard monetary policy (i.e. quantitative easing). Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 13,15,16 and 19); Greenspan (Ch's 5 & 8-10.)

7: International trade, finance and exchange rates (Lecture): Theory and evidence regarding the management of international trade, financial and exchange rate policies, with a particular focus on the European monetary union, Chinas influence on global markets and the US current account deficit. Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 21); Greenspan (Ch. 18)

8. Policy responses to financial crises (Lecture): A closer consideration of how central banks and finance ministries/treasuries should respond to financial crises, and a review of experiences with the 2008 financial crisis, the Asian financial crisis, and the great crash of 1929, among others. Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 20); Geithner; Krugman; Roubini; Reinhart

9. Government budget and debt management (Lecture): Reviews how fiscal policy is formulated in practice, touching on the impact of the political cycle, and how the Government manages its balance sheet, including consideration of the sustainability of public debt. Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 5 and 8); Reinhart
No Lecture, no tutorials (Mid-semester Break & Public Holiday)

10. Labour market policies and the macro-economy (Lecture): Considers how the labour market responds to macroeconomic developments and how labour market and other policies (e.g. taxation, social security) influence macroeconomic outcomes such as GDP growth, unemployment and inflation. Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 18)

11. Economic growth and development policies (Lecture): Introduction to modern/new growth theory, the so-called middle income trap, constraints on economic development and policies that promote productivity and growth. Readings/Ref: Acemoglu; Dornbusch (Ch. 7)

12. Case studies in macroeconomic policy Part I: (Lecture): International experience. Presents and draws lessons from several international macroeconomic policy case studies, including the Great Depression, US stagflation, British monetarism and Thatcherism, Greenspans put and quantitative easing. Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 20 and 21); Greenspan (esp. Chs 5 & 8-10)

13. Case studies in macroeconomic policy Part 2: (Lecture): Australian experience. Presents and draws lessons from several Australian macroeconomic policy case studies, including the Australia dollar float of 1983, twin deficits of the 1980s and 1990s, the 2008 financial crisis, and managing the mining boom and the transition at the end of it. Readings/Ref: Dornbusch (Ch. 20 and 21)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University’s assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment

Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

Feedback in this Course

A viewing session will be held for the mid semester and final exams. Further details will be posted on blackboard and bulk emailed to students at the relevant time.

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

School of Economics Assessment Guidelines

The act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another either intentionally or unintentionally. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

You must make it clear which ideas you have obtained from someone else. Superficial and minor changes do not disguise your use of the words of someone else. You commit plagiarism if you do not acknowledge the source of a direct quote, or a specific piece of writing that you have paraphrased, or even if you describe an idea or concept that you have heard or read somewhere without a reference or acknowledgement.

Examples of plagiarism are:

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence;

Direct copying of paragraphs, sentences, a single sentence or significant parts of a sentence with an end reference but without quotation marks around the copied text;

Copying ideas, concepts, research results, computer codes, statistical tables, designs, images, sounds or text or any combination of these;

Paraphrasing, summarising or simply rearranging another person's words, ideas, etc without changing the basic structure and/or meaning of the text;

Referring an idea or interpretation that is not one's own without identifying whose idea or interpretation it is;

A ‘cut and paste' of statements from multiple sources;

Presenting as independent, work done in collaboration with others;

Copying or adapting another student's original work into a submitted assessment item.

Submitting the same work or recycling work in more than one course without prior permission of the course coordinator.

Importance of Referencing

Complete and accurate referencing is essential for academic writing. There are three main reasons why full referencing is essential.

The first is that when another writer’s work is used without being referenced the act of plagiarism has been committed. Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one’s own work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another and is covered by the University’s Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy . 1. The penalty for plagiarism under the Policy can range from zero marks for the assessment item to cancellation of credit for the course.

The second reason for referencing is so that anyone reading the paper can follow up any ideas or concepts that have been presented. This is especially important in academic writing where people involved in research may read the paper. One of the main ways of researching a topic is to read what other people have written and then follow up some of the references they have cited. Then, after those ‘follow-up’ papers have been read the researcher can continue the search-trail by tracking down more references cited in those follow-up papers, and so on. This is known as a bibliographical chain. This research is difficult if not impossible to complete if papers are not fully and accurately referenced.

Thirdly, referencing is important as it adds credibility to the argument that you are presenting. If an argument is to have any credibility it must be supported by evidence and that evidence must be referenced.

If a reader of your paper can read something that you have written and ask questions like; who says? Or How do you know that? Or On what do you base that comment? Then what you have written needs to be referenced. For more information on the importance of, and direction on referencing please refer to the School of Economics Assignment Presentation Guide .

Consequences of Plagiarism

Students should be aware that the School of Economics does not hesitate to use the procedures available under the University of Queensland Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy to investigate suspected misconduct involving plagiarism.

The penalties imposed for cases where a finding of misconduct is made vary, based on the seriousness of the case and range across but are not limited to:

a reduction in the mark for the assessment piece or cancellation of the mark for the assessment piece;

a requirement to complete further or repeat work for the subject;

the cancellation of credit for the subject;

expulsion from the University.

Students should also be aware of the fact that when a finding of misconduct is made that this is recorded on the University’s central misconduct database.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
This course covers topics in modern macroeconomic policy at the intermediate level and builds significantly on the material covered in the prerequisite ECON2020 Macroeconomic Theory course. It begins with lectures on the origins of macroeconomic policymaking and the ongoing debates concerning the application of macroeconomic policy in different economic circumstances. Then there are lectures on the operation for fiscal, monetary, external and employment policies.

For generations, there have been disagreements about theory and policy. Some have proposed fine-tuning and others have rejected it. This course aims to equip you with a wide range of perspectives to help prepare you for problems and controversies you will likely encounter during your professional life. The course does not offer “right” answers that you can memorise. You will find out know who said what (including who agrees or disagrees with whom) , when they said it and why they said it.

It will be presumed that the technical material introduced in ECON2020, e.g. IS-LM-BP analysis, is fully understood both in delivery of the lectures and in assessment. Tutorials are not intended to be merely a means of reinforcing an earlier lecture; they are intended to be a forum for lively discussion and debate about topics where there is clear disagreement in the economics profession. It should be stressed that all prescribed reading material in textbook chapters and other readings will be assessable in examinations, even if not fully covered in the lectures. All required material will be clearly specified in class.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

This course is offered by Mr Gene Tunny for the first time so feedback is currently unavailable. We look forward to your response at the end of the semester.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
Lectures

Estimates of time for each topic may be approximate. Don't expect a topic to always fit into the scheduled lecture. If you miss a lecture, check on Blackboard under Learning Activities in the folder called Lecture Recordings to see exactly where we are up to.

Do try to attend all of the teaching sessions. No textbook contains all the assessable material in this course or presents it in an orderly way. So failure to attend lectures or tutorials may result in you being disadvantaged.

Some students, often because of work commitments, do not attend all lectures and unwisely defer viewing the Lecture Recordings until close to the test or exam. Falling a long way behind in this way often affects grades. And remember that the lecture recordings can fail sometimes.

Tutorials

Tutorials start in Week 2. Students are required to sign on promptly for a tutorial via mySI-net. T utorial attendance is highly recommended. Do not expect the tutorials to be like those provided in some other courses. Some tutorial questions will be basic, others will be advanced extensions or based on assessable selected readings not necessarily discussed at all in the lectures. If a t utorial goes beyond the relevant lecture, the material dealt with is also assessable in examinations.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Complete failure to demonstrate sufficient basic knowledge and understanding of key concepts, relationships and models. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 0-19.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Failure to demonstrate sufficient basic knowledge of key concepts, relationships and models. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 20-44.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Some basic knowledge is evident, but detail and precision are patently absent at a level sufficient to merit competency and therefore a passing grade. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 45-49.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A basic knowledge of core concepts and modelling techniques obtained. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 50-64.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

A more thorough and accurate understanding of modelling and policy analysis displayed through both written and verbal means. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 65-74.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

A thorough, accurate and comprehensive knowledge of concepts and model analysis displayed and some evidence of wider reading. Awarded for an overall mark in the range 75-84.99 percent inclusive.

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Accuracy and attention to detail displayed in all analysis together with significant evidence of critical evaluation of material and of wider reading. Requires an overall mark of 85 percent and above.

Other Requirements & Comments:

If you reach 50% at the end of the course you pass. You do not need to pass each component of the course to pass this course. You do not need to reach a pre-specified threshold of achievement for any single learning objective. Put simply, your grand total score is what matters.

In accordance with School of Economics policy on grades determination, percentage marks will NOT be rounded (e.g. 84.99 will remain as 84.99 and the student will be awarded a 6).
late_submission:
The assignment is due by 5pm Thursday 15 October .

Assignments submitted after the due date will be penalized unless prior agreement for late submission has been received from the Course Administrator. The penalty will be 5% of the total marks for the assignment will be deducted for assignments submitted after 5pm that day. If your assignment is submitted the next day, an additional 10% will be deducted, and an additional 10% will be deducted if it is submitted the day after that, and so on (including weekends) for the first 7 days after the due date; assignments not submitted 7 days after the due date will receive a mark of zero.

Computer failure and no back­ups are not acceptable reasons for lateness.

Requests for the granting of extensions must be made on the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and must be received by the Course Administrator at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. The request must also contain documentation satisfactorily supporting the request. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application and the student notified through their student email. An extension made on medical grounds will be for the number of days for which the medical certificate indicates the student was unwell, and will not exceed 14 days. Students who are ill for more than 14 days may wish to apply for withdrawal without academic penalty. The guidelines for late submission of progressive assessment, and the application form, may be accessed through myAdvisor.
other_assessment_information:
The School of Economics is committed to reducing the incidence of plagiarism. Further information on plagiarism and how to avoid an allegation of plagiarism is available under Section 6. Policies and Guidelines - 6.1. Assessment Related Policies & Guidelines of this Course Profile.

SUBMISSION OF ASSIGNMENT

The assignment must be submitted by the due date and time stated in the course profile. The assignment must be submitted electronically via Blackboard/TurnitIn. The instructions for submission are in the Online Submission Folder in your course Blackboard site.

The last page of your Assignment must consist of a signed and fully completed Economics Assignment Cover Sheet, available on the course Blackboard site.

Incomplete or incorrect assignment coversheets can cause delays in processing your assignments.

DEFERRED EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam: an In-Class Quiz, Mid-Semester or Final Exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a deferred examination at a later time.

Information on grounds for applying for deferred examinations can be viewed here:

http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2995

How to apply for a deferred exam can be found on the web at:

http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/deferred-application-2995

Please read this information very carefully before submitting your request.

Requests for deferred examinations and supporting documentation must be submitted online via mySI-net > myRequests tab

https://www.sinet.uq.edu.au/ps/uqsinetsignin.html

Requests for Deferral of Mid-Semester Examinations apply to examinations held either during class time or during the teaching weeks.

Requests for Deferral of End of Semester examinations apply to examinations held at the end of semester during the University’s examination period.

If you are approved a deferred examination, you must be available to sit the deferred examination at the scheduled time. The deferred examination would be your final opportunity to attempt the examination, as you cannot again defer your deferred examination.

Application rulings :

Application rulings will be notified to you by email to your University student email address. Should you not receive a response to your application for an end of semester deferred examination by the time the final results are released, please contact Examinations Section or your faculty, as appropriate.

SUPPLEMENTARY ASSESSMENT

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of three), you may be eligible to sit a supplementary exam. You can find information about supplementary assessment at: http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2954 . Read this information very carefully to see if you may be eligible to apply for supplementary assessment. Also see General Award Rules, Section 1A.17.3, and your program rules.

Applications for supplementary assessment must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 days of the official release of results .

Important Information regarding Supplementary Examinations for courses within the Faculty of Business, Economics & Law:

Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

Students are advised to take the Deferred & Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (16 – 18 December 2015).

Students with approved deferred/supplementary examinations are expected to be available on these days, no deferrals or off-campus sittings will be granted.
